title: Practicing Radical Acceptance Step by Step   Observe that you are questioning or fighting reality (“It shouldn’t be this way”). 

pages.text:
344
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 11b   (Distress Tolerance Worksheets 9, 9a)
Practicing Radical Acceptance Step by Step

 Observe that you are questioning or fighting reality (“It shouldn’t be this way”).

 Remind yourself that the unpleasant reality is just as it is and cannot be changed (“This is what
happened”).

 Remind yourself that there are causes for the reality. Acknowledge that some sort of history led
up to this very moment. Consider how people’s lives have been shaped by a series of factors.
Notice that given these causal factors and how history led up to this moment, this reality had to
occur just this way (“This is how things happened”).

 Practice accepting with the whole self (mind, body, and spirit). Be creative in finding ways
to involve your whole self. Use accepting self-talk—but also consider using relaxation;
mindfulness of your breath; half-smiling and willing hands while thinking about what feels
unacceptable; prayer; going to a place that helps bring you to acceptance; or imagery.

 Practice opposite action. List all the behaviors you would do if you did accept the facts. Then
act as if you have already accepted the facts. Engage in the behaviors that you would do if you
really had accepted.

 Cope ahead with events that seem unacceptable. Imagine (in your mind’s eye) believing what
you don’t want to accept. Rehearse in your mind what you would do if you accepted what
seems unacceptable.

 Attend to body sensations as you think about what you need to accept.

 Allow disappointment, sadness, or grief to arise within you.

 Acknowledge that life can be worth living even when there is pain.

 Do pros and cons if you find yourself resisting practicing acceptance.

Practicing Radical Acceptance Step by Step

 Observe that you are questioning or fighting reality (“It shouldn’t be this way”).

 Remind yourself that the unpleasant reality is just as it is and cannot be changed (“This is what
happened”).

 Remind yourself that there are causes for the reality. Acknowledge that some sort of history led

Notice that given these causal factors and how history led up to this moment, this reality had to
occur just this way (“This is how things happened”).

 Practice accepting with the whole self (mind, body, and spirit). Be creative in finding ways
to involve your whole self. Use accepting self-talk—but also consider using relaxation;

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Practicing Radical Acceptance Step by Step   Observe that you are questioning or fighting reality (“It shouldn’t be this way”). 
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
